* It may not be good to always build both diag code and choose which one to use at runtime
    * For a usual RCP, the native diag code is totally useless. On some SoCs, our RCP application FW just fits in the size limit. So we should avoid adding code to normal RCP as possible.
    * This makes the code logic more complex. For example, we may need to add a flag in the Diags module to tell if it should use the native diag code or RCP diag code. And this applys to both Diags modules (Host and RCP). That says, we need to ensure the flag is correct on both host and RCP. We can use configuration as a default value for the flag. But this goes back, we still add one more config. 
* I tried a linking solution: move different diag code to two new source files and build two different `openthread-radio` library (most things are common except for some diag and radio_callback code). This should work. One flaw is that those class members (only for native diag code) will be there anyway. (I cannot think of a way to avoid this, is there any trick to do that without adding any configuration?)
* I think the best way is to add a new build type for openthread core (For example, `OPENTHREAD_RADIO_ALONE`, which is a sibling of `OPENTHREAD_FTD`, `OPENTHREAD_MTD` and `OPENTHREAD_RADIO`). And we will also build a different lib `openthread-radio-alone` and let ot-cli-rcp link this lib. This is much simpler.

As for this suggestion, it may not be obvious why I asked to do this compared to other tests in pyresample. A lot of the existing tests in pyresample are following bad practice. Additionally a lot of the tests are written with the standard library's `unittest` which is not as flexible compared to pytest which we've been using more and more throughout the pytroll libraries. In general, we want to keep clean and short test methods so they are easy to understand and easy to modify if needed. This should result in less duplicated code if the tests all depend on the same helper functions (for example, to create fake data). It also helps in seeing at a glance what is failing and why if the test has a single purpose. For example, if I run the pyresample tests and your original function fails, I don't know (initially) if the first chunk failed or the second. And even if I did I have to read the source code and understand the entire test function to understand how the various sections differ. The name of the test function/method is usually shown when there is a failure so if I see that and it is descriptive I can usually get a basic idea of what is going on and why the tests are failing.

What scaries me is that the ndarray that is passed could also be CHW for some reason, and the function would just return something wrong.

For that reason, we could try to make the scope of this function to be as narrow as possible, so that we can be sure we won't be mishandling user inputs.
PIL Images and AccImage have a well-defined format representation (although I'm not sure that many people use AccImage actually), which is not the case for ndarrays which are generic data containers.

This runs into some issues:

1) The `output` is a misnomer. We are not returning `Sources` or `PythonSources` etc. We are always returning `HydratedSources` no matter what.
2) Doesn't work well with "aggregator" rules like we have in `determine_source_files.py`. Very often, we will want to allow multiple distinct `Sources` classes; for example, https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/pull/9638 allows `files()`, `resources()`, and Python targets. Asserting that `type(input) == output` means that the aggregator rule couldn't ergonomically support multiple disjoint `Sources` types like it currently does.

https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/fdc097576a0ef0dd1d3eeb0ebb535bf1b2da3887/src/python/pants/core/util_rules/determine_source_files.py#L35-L38

3. Some call sites explicitly do not want to use codegen, e.g. `filedeps`. Setting `HydrateSourcesRequest(sources_field, codegen_language=None)` expresses this much more clearly than `HydrateSourcesRequest(sources_field, output=Sources)` and relying on the implicit knowledge that there should be no code generators that go from `ThriftSources -> Sources` (which isn't ever enforced).

I purposefully avoided using assertTrueEventually in this case, because I want to make sure that I receive exactly 1 event and not more than that. I think assertTrueEventually lets the test pass as soon as you receive that 1 event, but I want to wait to see I don't receive the event from all of the listeners to make sure smart listener works local only.

The main thing to understand is that `objc_setAssociatedObject` is just a global function allowing you to specify values for any pair of object instance and "key", where the "key" is just a `void*` value. It's global, so any other code in the same process can call the same function to access the values associated with a given object instance. The safest way to guarantee that some other code doesn't use the same key in a different way is to use a key that no one else knows about. Using `@selector` only satisfies this requirement if the name of the selector is something no one else knows about. A static variable by definition is unique, even if another .m or .cpp file uses the exact same name.

I would lean towards opposing this change on the grounds that the purpose of `__repr__` is to provide a "real" representation (hence its name) of the object, that is useful for development and debugging - or, to quote one StackOverflow user, " `__repr__` is for developers, `__str__` is for customers" (See full answers in https://stackoverflow.com/questions/1436703/what-is-the-difference-between-str-and-repr). In this sense, hiding the presence of `temp_storage`, for example, runs counter to the purpose of `__repr__` and you can easily imagine such a decision getting in the way of someone trying to debug (that could be us!).

If you want a human-readable "pretty printed" output, that's the job of `__str__`, not `__repr__`. One thing I will concede is that `Conversation` and `Speaker` do not implement `__str__`, so that's certainly something we can fix (`Utterance` already implements `__str__`)

In this multistage solution, we do not carry over *any* potential residual components from the build process. That is very hard to achieve with RUN in a single stage. There is always junk left behind and not to mention it is hard to read unmaintainable. There is also observational bias to this commonality of long-winded single-stage builds: nobody dares to change anything because they do not understand the consequences of their changes. Not the best practice, that is not a place where we want to be.

At first, that can seem like a reasonable fear that a generic install script could install something outside the defined install location. To overcome our fear it is enough to open up the install script to see what it does: pip install exclusively. While it could be possible for a setup.py to do something incredibly stupid here the python pip framework is reasonably opinionated on what can and should be done (too opinionated if you ask me). If any files are installed they will be put in the target specified by pip configuration.

Do you feel strong about the /usr/local copy I might be able to fix that. But if you are against the multi-stage I cannot fix that. That is a requirement for a better more maintainable Dockerfile with a smaller build artifact that also can be generated more quickly.

as a matter of course when doing tests like these I really like to do a pattern like this:

```
    executed = {}

    class MyIOManager(IOManager):
        def handle_output(self, context, obj):
            ...

        def load_input(self, context):
            executed["yes"] = True
            assert context.asset_key == asset1.key
            return 5
      
      # later
      
      assert executed["yes"] 
```

Just to ensure that code the framework calls back into is actually called. I've caught more than one bug this way and it proves beyond all doubt to future code readers that the rest is working properly. More than once I've found test suites that asserted against success only and it turns out they have not configured things right to call back into the right user code.

There are two approaches to generate ids for providers that do not provide native ids in the records:
1) Generate a base64 encoding of a string of kind `type/name`
2) Generate a hash of record content (example: https://github.com/AnalogJ/lexicon/blob/bd347b59b0f0ab098ecb8169e1e3609b09a90935/lexicon/providers/godaddy.py#L285-L291)

For 1), I prefer a hash over the string itself because it gives identifiers more transportable, since base64 is primarly designed for that.

You could add a tiny dispatcher that you delegate this common task to that is DRY and also explicit:

```python
def desoto(self):
    """calculate desoto model"""
    return self._model_dispatcher(self.system.calcparams_desoto, 'desoto')

def pvsyst(self):
    """calculate pvsyst model"""
    return self._model_dispatcher(self.system.calcparams_pvsyst, 'pvsyst')

def _model_dispatcher(self, model_calcparams, model_name=None):
    """
    Delegate that dispatches the model

    Parameters
    ----------
    model_calcparams : function
        A function that calculates the models parameters given irradiance and cell temperature.
    model_name : str
        Optional string that may be used to further distinguish the model in the future.
    """
    (photocurrent, saturation_current, resistance_series,
     resistance_shunt, nNsVth) = (
        model_calcparams(self.effective_irradiance,
                                      self.temps['temp_cell']))

    self.diode_params = (photocurrent, saturation_current,
                         resistance_series,
                         resistance_shunt, nNsVth)

    self.dc = self.system.singlediode(
        photocurrent, saturation_current, resistance_series,
        resistance_shunt, nNsVth)

    self.dc = self.system.scale_voltage_current_power(self.dc).fillna(0)

    return self
```

I have two **new** observations for the `CRPIX` issue:

1. This code will probably create a WCS that is shifted by 1 pixel. You _are_ computing SIP correctly (at least as I can see so far), but the FITS WCS is wrong. You are computing and using `CRPIX` in your computations as a `0-based` value but the FITS header expects `1-based` value. Therefore, I think, the simplest fix would be:

```suggestion
        hdr['CRPIX1'] = crpix1 + 1
        hdr['CRPIX2'] = crpix2 + 1
```

2. Because you did not caught it with your current test, I would suggest adding a test specifically designed for this: when at `CRPIX` wcs transformation should return `CRVAL`. Essentially you want to check (`allclose`):
```python
w.wcs_pix2world(*w.wcs.crpix, 1) == w.wcs.crval  # allclose instead of equality
```

There is an unspoken design principle I had in mind when writing this C interface; let's add an Issue to have me add that to some developer docs. The principle was that, to the extent possible, we should take a C++ library that follows the "call-method-and-mutate" approach that's similar to Python's own `list.sort()` and to how PyEphem works, and that instead presents a modern functional interface where object attributes are generally immutable and where methods return information rather than leaving the object with visibly mutated state, more like the modern `sorted()` routine that has replaced `list.sort()` in modern Python code.

@tommy-waltmann @klywang This change is awkward because it means we'll stop providing wheels for Python 3.7 but continue providing Python 3.6 wheels... Hmm. A gap like that is unusual/unexpected. The Python 3.7 cibuildwheel job fails at the _testing_ stage (after the wheel is built) when it installs the pinned dependencies from [requirements/requirements-test.txt](https://github.com/glotzerlab/freud/blob/master/requirements/requirements-test.txt). CI output here: https://github.com/glotzerlab/freud/runs/4680663466

I suggest one of the following courses of action:
- Add a new requirements file that is not pinned to "newest" but instead uses `>=min_required_version` for all dependencies. It would be an exact clone of [.circleci/ci-oldest-reqs.txt](https://github.com/glotzerlab/freud/blob/master/.circleci/ci-oldest-reqs.txt) but with `>=` instead of `==`. We could call it `requirements/requirements-test-compatible.txt` or something like that. Dependabot won't update `>=` pinnings so it wouldn't be changed like the other requirements files. This file's pinnings would be used for Python 3.7 (or more generally, any Python versions that are dropped by NEP 29 but we still wish to test using compatible-but-not-oldest dependency versions).
- End freud's support for Python 3.6 and 3.7, in accordance with NEP 29. We would update our "oldest" pinnings to be the lowest versions supported by Python 3.8 (we shouldn't eliminate the "oldest" CI because it ensures compatibility with older NumPy and CMake versions, among other dependencies).
- Drop cibuildwheel _tests_ for Python 3.7 but keep the CI job - cibuildwheel would only _build_ the wheel, and we would rely on the other (newest, oldest) Python versions to ensure that the wheels are adequately tested.

In this case, my adding an assert here was just lazy, because there _is_ a possible path for a user to get here. They could create voice leading quartets made up of totally empty intervals. So mypy found a corner case bug, and I was too lazy to add a proper exception. As opposed to the one below about `common.isIterable` where the so-called failure case is not reachable because of what we know about `isIterable()`, that a boolean should never pass that function (it's music21's very own type-narrowing function).

To think aloud just to check my own understanding. (Not to persuade, because let's definitely not add things that you find annoying!)

The `assert` doesn't bother me as an articulation of an assumption the author relies on down the line. ("Somewhere else in the module is responsible for making this not None. But that's forty lines away, and if it ever gets refactored, let's add safety here, because I'm about to start accessing attributes on it.") As opposed to a failure path that is part of normal execution that a user might actually need to know about, care about, and catch. And I think part and parcel with `no-implication-that-users-should-know-or-care` is why you can run python in `-O` to skip them. They're not necessarily even net negatives for user feedback, if the author is proven wrong and the "never supposed to happen" turns out to happen--as it's better to get `AssertionError: self.stream is None`, which articulates the root cause, instead of `AttributeError: self.stream has no attribute __iter__` 400 lines later, which obscures it. Yes, more messages for the user is always nice, but this is the tool for those gray areas "no, this can't fail, but it wouldn't be safe later if something else changes". If there is a path for a user to reach it, then assert is not the tool.

In a few weeks I can spruce these up to disable/raise instead.

I think the value of `mypy` is not just "did it find bugs in my functionality" but maybe more importantly for us "did it find bugs in my type annotations".  On a project with community contributions, it should save a future maintainer time to make sure folks aren't adding in new bad annotations.

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import numpy as np
from numpy.testing import assert_allclose

def mean_squared_distance(input, target, reduction='mean', w=None):
    out = np.square(input - target)
    if w is not None:
        out = np.multiply(out, w)
    if reduction == 'mean':
        out = np.mean(out)
    elif reduction == 'sum':
        out = np.sum(out)
    return out

loss = nn.MSELoss()
input = torch.randn(3, 5)
target = torch.randn(3, 5)
output = loss(input, target)

my_loss = mean_squared_distance(input.numpy(), target.numpy())
assert_allclose(my_loss, output.numpy(), rtol=1e-05)

Depends on what semantics we're going for. Like if you have a dict property and you run a plan to go from`{a: 1, b: 2}` to `{a: 1, c: 3}` is that recording "update to `{a: 1, c: 3}`" or "delete key b, add key c with 3"? Because the former only allows a plan to execute if it ends up with the value `{a: 1, c: 3}` but the later would allow someone to come and change the dict to something like `{b: 2, d: 4}` and the plan would allow the value `{c: 3, d: 4}` but not allow the value `{a: 1, c: 3}`.

Might be that second semantic is useful but it seemed more complex to code and explain and I don't have a concrete scenario that would use it so I err'ed on simplicity.

I agree that multicast is very handy, but I have one question about this:

- How is CAN arbitration handled? Is there any specific order in which CAN messages are received by all participating CAN "controllers" on the multicast IP bus? For example, in a real life CAN bus, CAN messages have an arbitration function, so CAN messages with a lower ID have higher priority. Also, on real hardware CAN messages are received in the same order at all CAN controllers. This might seem details, so I'm not sure how important this is for simulation purpose. But it might trigger some unexpected situations when higher level protocols make some assumptions about this.

As a side note: I created [virtualcan](https://github.com/windelbouwman/virtualcan), in which CAN bus arbitration is not yet implemented, but since this project does take the central server approach, it is possible to add priority sorting, and messages will arrive at all ends in the same order.

It might be good to document how the priority / ordering of CAN message is handled so that people know what to expect.

Sure, I agree that two features of this task are not entirely orthogonal. And I was worried about that at first.

Yes: you're unlikely to use the multi-object and the WHERE features together. 

But trying to make the two features work together nicely very quickly turns into a messy science project. Something like:

objects=account:name LIKE '%foo%',contact:name='foo'

I felt it was better to just let users pick whether they want to take advantage of the multi-object mode or the WHERE mode. If in some obscure case they happen to work together properly for you then that's fine as well.

One can always achieve multi-object deletes like this (using simplified Robot syntax):

Run Task   DeleteData    objects=account   criteria=name LIKE '%foo%
Run Task   DeleteData    objects=contact    criteria=name='foo'

The performance will be almost identical, I think, because the DeleteData task itself is just a loop over the object types.

`make testall` runs the test matrix with `tox`, as well as linters, documentation building, testing in example projects. `make test` just runs python tests in the current environment.

I think `make test` is good to get quick feedback on a change, since it only runs the test suite. `make testall` is slower, but provides a more complete feedback to contributors. Recommending `make testall` before submitting a PR helps spotting as many mistakes as possible.

Regarding using `make test` for tox, I’m slightly in favor of keeping the current `make test` for the quick feedback and `make testall` for the complete report. Mostly because `make testall` is a good name for running the complete test matrix and lint, docs, examples, and `make test` is less typing to just get a quick feedback on a current change. I can easily be convinced otherwise though.

> Its strange to have a field be inherently required when its only the use case that can ever really say so.

Well, to be clear, the target author is still making the ultimate decision. They choose whichever fields make sense when declaring their `core_fields`.

If a target author likes some field, minus that it was or was not required, they can easily subclass the original and the API will support that.

For example, we want to create a new `text_files` target that behaves the same as `files()`, including Pants not chrooting the sources, but we want to use default globs of `*.txt` for `sources`. Because we have default globs, we're fine if the user leaves off `sources` in the BUILD file.

```python
class TextSources(FileSources):
  required = False
  default = ("*.txt",)

class TextFiles(Target):
  alias = "text_files"
  core_fields = tuple({*Files.core_fields, TextSources} - {FilesSources})

tgt = TextFiles({}, address=...)
assert tgt[TextSources] == tgt[FilesSources] == tgt[Sources]
```

--

Typed fields are a major paradigm shift away from V1 and a Target-driven API. I can see why it would be weird for a field to determine if it can or cannot be left out of a BUILD file, but that is the spirit of the Target API. 

One of the goals of the project is for all of the logic to move to Fields such that Target definitions do no more than a) register their alias and b) pick which fields they want from the buffet of available fields, including any new fields the author may have written.

This means, for example, that we can put methods on the fields themselves, e.g. this method on `Timeout`:

https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/e0c08cf7e2180bf2ea6a29bdf646fa5f13b0fd82/src/python/pants/backend/python/rules/targets.py#L128-L140

Conceptually/spiritually, the mindset is "I trust the Field to know what makes sense, e.g. what values it works with, whether it has a default value, whether it has extra methods to do something meaningful with its value", etc. If a Target doesn't like that Field, it can create a new Field.

(I can speak more to _why_ this is important to the project if it'd help. The above comment takes a first pass and https://docs.google.com/document/d/1nxPdvuzgCPKhTabhfYBN2tbcr85enSR83OpcvdfWXfY/edit#heading=h.6o3hd2sgs0dd explains most of it, but it is a major paradigm shift so I'm happy to explain in different words the why.)

--

One other point that came up in discussion with Benjy: to be clear, "optional"/"required" is an overloaded term in this discussion. 

The existence of the field is not optional - if the target type registers `Compatibility`, it will always have that field defined and we will always be able to say `tgt[Compatibility]`

What is optional is whether the user explicitly passes a value in the BUILD file for that field or if we allow them to rely on a default value.

I simply want [my/your/any-cloudpickle-contributor/maintainer] future-self to be able to quickly understand why a specific line/commit exists, and this through atomic unit tests (we can also have composite ones, but to me atomic ones are **necessary**), related to the exact feature we decided to introduce in the said line/commit. In our case, this PR introduces support for pickling classes **subclassing** `typing.Generic` constructs. I think we should have an atomic unit test for this feature. 
- If we are missing tests testing the pickling of `typing.Generic` constructs only, let's add another test.
- If we are missing tests testing the pickling of the `globals` of the `typing` module, let's add another test.
- If we are missing composite tests testing the pickling what's inside a dynamic class/function `__annotations__`  (be it `Generic` construct or other `typing` constructs), let's add another test.
- If we want to test any combination of the 4 situations above (the first one being subclassing `typing.Generic`), then let's add another test.

 But why mixing everything up in one single test? The test suite of `cloudpickle` stills run pretty fast (<1 minute against `CPython`), not sure there is a need for optimization on this end yet.

My concern here is that a library based on TorchVision registers something for our builtin stuff and thus invalidates our docs if we import the library. Imagine a library does this

```python
@F.register_kernel(F.resize, datapoints.Image)
def lol(...):
    ...
```

I can already see a user of that library coming to our issue tracker with something like

> I see these weird behavior for resize that is not consistent with the docs. Here is my env

And after some back and forth we find that they have `import third_party_library` at the top of his script.

I want to prevent this. Especially since with the subclass check, there is no reason for it. The third-party library can simply do

```python
class CustomImage(datapoints.Image):
    ...

@F.register_kernel(F.resize, CustomImage)
def kernel(...):
    ...
```

Now, if the user deals with plain `datapoints.Image`'s they will always get our behavior.

> You set all markers to the minimal possible double value so that when the actual watershed algorithm starts drawing from the heap, new pixels will always be sorted after the initial markers, regardless of their pixel value?

Yes.

> In that case, I am really curious about the intended effect here. At a first glance it seems un-intuitive to me why markers are a special case whose neighborhood must be evaluated first. 

The watersheding of a marker $p$ will only label pixels with values greater than their own, if you don't set the $p$'s value to $-\infty$ it won't label a neighboring pixel $q$ with lower-value when there's a competing marker $t$ with a path to $q$ lower than $p$'s value. The intended behavior is that the water from the markers should drip down the bottom of neighboring basins (neighboring pixels with lower values).

For reference, [[1]](https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Serge-Beucher/publication/230837870_The_Morphological_Approach_to_Segmentation_The_Watershed_Transformation/links/00b7d5319b26f3ffa2000000/The-Morphological-Approach-to-Segmentation-The-Watershed-Transformation.pdf), Section IV A 4, $$W_{-1}(g) = M$$ The markers are set to level -1 (they consider the image has non-negative values).
This is shown in Figure 16, where the `modified function` (hatched region) is zero on the markers (selected minima).

I changed the compact watershed test again and added a "barrier" with a greater value than the markers' regions, so the result is not an implementation detail as before (tie-zones). I explain it in the test documentation.

If a CLI command makes several MSAL calls, only the first telemetry record is preserved.

The most special command is `az login` which calls `acquire_token_interactive` first, then several subsequent `acquire_token_silent_with_error`s:

1. `acquire_token_interactive`: initial login
2. `acquire_token_silent_with_error`: list tenants
3. `acquire_token_silent_with_error`: list subscriptions in tenant 1
4. `acquire_token_silent_with_error`: list subscriptions in tenant 2
5. ...

If step 1 fails, its telemetry will be sent and `az login` will fail. If step 1 succeeds, its telemetry will be sent, and step 2 and following steps' telemetry will be discarded, no matter if they fail or not.

For long running operation commands such as `az storage account create`, only the MSAL telemetry for the initial `PUT` request is sent. Following MSAL telemetry for `GET` polling requests is discarded.

2. `test_safe_url_string_url` is a test that takes input and output of `safe_url_string()` from `SAFE_URL_URL_CASES` and expects that output unless the input is in `KNOWN_SAFE_URL_STRING_URL_ISSUES`.
3. Hence, `KNOWN_SAFE_URL_STRING_URL_ISSUES` is basically a list of cases that should xfail.
4. `SAFE_URL_URL_CASES` says that `"http://[2a01:5cc0:1:2:3:4]"` should give ValueError and this is true only on the new Python, so this change removes xfail for it on the new Python.
5. `SAFE_URL_URL_CASES` says that ```safe_url_string('https://"%;<=>@[]^`{|}\x7f:"%;<=>@[]^`{|}\x7f:@example.com')``` should be encoded correctly and that works only on older Python, so this change adds xfail for it on the new Python.

It's a tricky example because the difference between the radius and the distance between their center changes the boundary of the results.

My understanding is the following, for two adjacent but not overlapping circles, the markers can have an offset of, at most, the difference between their radii. If it's greater than the radii difference, the neighbors around the marker of the larger circle won't let it propagate before the smaller circle. Note that, this problem is specific to when using EDT for the watershed, and when there's overlap it gets much trickier because it depends on how the EDT changes with the overlap.

At least, in the fact that we're trying to offer `uint64` interfaces as well as the otherwise standard string interface.

In that context, my thinking is that the fundamentally "correct" and unambiguous representation of an `H3Index` is its `uint64` representation. I realize that the `str` representation is the one people are used to, which is why I was trying to strike a balance in this error message. 

It's **correct** in the sense that the error is saying "The integer `0x...` that i received is not a valid H3Index", but it is **familiar** in the sense that a user sees the hex representation.

Suppose, for example, a user comes to this library at some point in the future and only uses the `numpy_int` interface. When they see an error that hex `'f'` is invalid, they might think "What!? I never put in 'f'!", but if they see that hex `0xf` is invalid, they have a better idea that it is talking about an int.

Yeah, one can constrain it to only apply to `@rule`-annotated functions:  using `pattern-inside`: https://semgrep.dev/playground/s/ejQd

```yaml
rules:
  - id: use-pure-path
    patterns:
      - pattern-inside: |
          @rule
          def $FUNC(...):
            ...
      - pattern: pathlib.Path
    message: >-
      Don't use pathlib.Path, use pathlib.PurePath instead: file IO should happen via the engine, not Python
    languages:
      - python
    severity: ERROR
```

```python
from pathlib import Path

@rule
def foo():
    Path("inside")

def bar():
    Path("outside")

Path("also outside")
```

That'll only flag `Path("inside")`. That said, for Pants' actual use case, I think we can have helper functions/types that aren't `@rule`s that still should use `PurePath`, not `Path`.

```python
)
```

Specifically, the `raise <type>, <value>, <traceback>` form doesn't exist any more in favor of `raise <value>.with_traceback(<traceback>)`. 

Python3 is better about retaining the correct info anyway, so given the following function:

In python3:
``` python
def raiser():
    raise Exception("foo")
```
These two behave basically the same:
``` python
try:
    raiser()
except Exception as e:
    raise e.with_traceback(sys.exc_info()[2])

try:
    raiser()
except Exception as e:
    raise e
```

The traceback for both of these implementations clearly shows where the exception originated from:
```
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
Exception                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-4-fefcf4ed47ea> in <module>
      2     raiser()
      3 except Exception as e:
----> 4     raise e.with_traceback(sys.exc_info()[2])
      5

<ipython-input-4-fefcf4ed47ea> in <module>
      1 try:
----> 2     raiser()
      3 except Exception as e:
      4     raise e.with_traceback(sys.exc_info()[2])
      5

<ipython-input-3-bb248eb5a561> in raiser()
      1 def raiser():
----> 2     raise Exception("foo")
      3

Exception: foo
```

In python 2, the `raise e` form drops the original traceback
``` python
try:
    raiser()
except Exception as e:
    raise e
```

So you can't see any info in the traceback about what line originated the exception.
```
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
Exception                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-14-5981f9f962cd> in <module>()
      2     raiser()
```

In order to get that behavior in python2, you need to use this form:
``` python
try:
    raiser()
except Exception as e:
    type_, value, tb = sys.exc_info()
    raise type_, value, tb
```

You get a better traceback, though less complete than the python3 version:
```
---------------------------------------------------------------------------
Exception                                 Traceback (most recent call last)
<ipython-input-13-7a5b66c805f2> in <module>()
      1 try:
----> 2     raiser()
      3 except Exception as e:
      4     type_, value, tb = sys.exc_info()
      5     raise type_, value, tb

<ipython-input-9-8127c176ebf6> in raiser()
      1 def raiser():
----> 2     raise Exception("foo")

Exception: foo
```

`with_traceback` doesn't exist in python2, so you need the 3-tuple form to get this behavior.

`request` is a magic Pytest argument to access the “test context”. `request.addfinalizer` is a common pattern to [register clean up code](https://docs.pytest.org/en/latest/fixture.html#fixture-finalization-executing-teardown-code); this fixture essentially calls `reset` after every test to ensure the environment is clean.

There are a few reasons. Generally, eager failure is easier than deferred failure. If a module must exist and work for any environment, the error of top-most inportings will be raised at module importing time. Otherwise it will be raised in deep side of call stacks which can be missed by low-coverage test suites. I prefer not to create local import unless there is a circular dependency or huge performance drop by importing.
In this case, another matter is the advantage. time is a standard and widely used module which must be used in pytest and ring. So the advantage from local import is very low.

I would say "let's add more assertions!" ;-)  They are good, and possibly a time/life saver if there is an inherent assumption in the code (e.g. that trial onsets are non-descending, that duplicate information about durations is congruent across different places, etc) which drives the code logic.  They allow to catch problematic usecases which invalidate the assumptions without silently providing incomplete or bogus results.

> They behave differently based on optimization level.

Yes, if you run via `python -O` assertion statements are simply ignored and no sane code "upstairs" should catch and treat AssertionError to guide its logic (we are, khe khe, to [get rid of those in datalad](https://github.com/datalad/datalad/pull/2832)].   In my experience I have never saw any notable performance boost by running with -O so I stopped using it (in PyMVPA we were somewhat meticulous about assertions and `__debug__` modes, but never saw pragmatic benefit). I see. Previously we queued app started events when the writer was enabled. Now we have to wait until we collect all possible start up errors before sending this event. This can be tricky.

I am not sure if there is an easy way to determine if an application has "successfully started".

Suggestion: We submit an app-started event after the first trace is sent to the agent. Any errors queued after the first trace is submitted will not be considered a start up error and will be ignored by the telemetry writer. The con of this approach is that we need to delay sending all telemetry events until a trace is sent. We can not send metrics/logs/integrations/dependencies/etc until the `app-started` event is ready. This approach could result in a memory leak. A workaround could be to set a max size on the telemetry queue but then we will drop metrics/logs/....

2. You may want to specify a different default value for an empty database to the trie, so that it can be used for different characteristics. Indeed, both XML-RPC and JSON-RPC are pretty flexible on HTTP status code returned.

XML-RPC states that (see http://xmlrpc.com/spec.md section "Response format"):
> Unless there's a lower-level error, always return 200 OK.

JSON-RPC, as it was designed to be compatible with any protocol (not only HTTP) (see https://www.jsonrpc.org/specification#overview) doesn't imply a specific status code or another (see https://www.jsonrpc.org/specification#response_object). But the behavior of batch request specifically mention that the server must return an array with all results, including error payloads when needed (see https://www.jsonrpc.org/specification#batch)

I understand that behavior is not ideal but it is most a limitation of both standards than an implementation issue.

The task-per-param-and-fold Spark option isn't hard, but in the end I suspect it's slower than your current approach because Spark has to shuffle a bunch of data. Off the top of my head, it's just this (in Scala, easier for me to write off the top of my head) for what it's worth:

```
val numParams = parameter_iterable.size()
val indexedTrainTest = sc.parallelize(cv.split(X, y, groups).zipWithIndex()
val indexedParams = sc.parallelize(parameter_iterable).zipWithIndex()
indexedTrainTest.cartesian(indexedParams).map { case (((train, test), t), (param, p)) =>
  val index = (t * numParams + p, (param, train, test))
  ...
}
```

The task-per-param Spark version where all folds are considered in one task also isn't hard:

```
val numParams = parameter_iterable.size()
val indexedParams = sc.parallelize(parameter_iterable).zipWithIndex()
val trainTestBroadcast = sc.broadcast(cv.split(X, y, groups).zipWithIndex())
indexedParams.flatMap { case (param, p) =>
  trainTestBroadcast.value.map { case ((train, test), t) =>
      val index = (t * numParams + p, (param, train, test))
      ...
  }
}
```

I'm OK sticking to your approach right now as it doesn't change the distribution of the data and maximizes parallelism, at the cost of some overhead.

If re-splitting the data adds, say, 5 seconds when the data set is about 10m records, then that's non-trivial but still small relative to the overhead of Spark and the model fitting itself, which probably runs at least tens of seconds at that scale.

In the previous system is was impossible to keep a string on the java side for any length of time.  Even if you you were just passing around a large string from java to java, it was forced to come back to python.  There was a global switch that was supposed to keep java strings on the java side, but it was filled with bugs.  The revised version is exactly as the switch state was supposed to do.  You call a method and get back a java.lang.String and if you want to pay to convert it call str() on it.

The previous string behavior was very much regrettable.   It was impossible prototype simple string codes because methods things were the user clearly wanted to get back a java string like substring() instead gave pystring instead.  

Mode switching like we had was impossible to maintain for any realistic use  (so that one was out) and our previous default and getting pystring every time a string prohibited a number of patterns.  

Can it be undone, yes (with effort).  In the code returning jvalues back from a method call and in the field string we can add a special case that forces the string to decode back to python object.  As the path string path is no longer internally different than  any other handler (part of the code simplification), we would need to check each of the paths.   

So the question is what will this break.  
- If the user got a string  and expected it to have python methods, it fails with method not found.
- Jstrings convert themselves automatically when going to string concatenation or formatting so no breakage here.
- left equivalence and right equivalence  are not equal.  Jstring knows how to compare to a pystring properly, but pystring does not know how to compare to jstring. 
- Use as dictionary keys, does not work as expected especially if mixed.
    
So as to what you were asking."is it mandatory."  I think so, the previous design left a lot of bad behavior in which ever mode was selected by the global switch.   We can special case it to push this down the road and make old code but it still leaves a lot of holes in the API.  I tested all of my code base with the new behavior and found only two places that needed a str() call added.

I probably should have opened the context a bit, but I'll do it here.
So if we have a config file `/etc/apache2/apache2.conf` with the following contents:
```
# First comment line
# Second comment line
WhateverDirective whatevervalue
<ABlock>
    DirectiveInABlock dirvalue
</ABlock>
SomeDirective somedirectivevalue
<ABlock>
    AnotherDirectiveInABlock dirvalue
</ABlock>
# Yet another comment
```
It translates to augeas tree nodes as follows, when calling `aug.match("/files/etc/apache2/apache2.conf/*")`:

```
[
"/files/etc/apache2/apache2.conf/#comment[1]",
"/files/etc/apache2/apache2.conf/#comment[2]",
"/files/etc/apache2/apache2.conf/directive[1]",
"/files/etc/apache2/apache2.conf/ABlock[1]",
"/files/etc/apache2/apache2.conf/directive[2]",
"/files/etc/apache2/apache2.conf/ABlock[2]",
"/files/etc/apache2/apache2.conf/#comment[3]"
]
```
So regardless of directive name, its key in the Augeas tree is always "directive", same goes with the comments, and all of them have their own respective indices. Blocks however have their actual name in the Augeas path.

In this comment with generalizing I meant "hiding" the actual name of the directive or a comment.

Why not Iterable? Is it because Iterables are not guaranteed to have a length, and `len(obj)` fails with an exception?

If that's the case, then a more appropriately narrow guard would be `isinstance(obj, Iterable) and isinstance(obj, Sized)`. In the [Python collections ABCs](https://docs.python.org/3/library/collections.abc.html),

   * Iterable just means that it has `__iter__` (the `for x in obj` loops will work)
   * Sized just means that it has a `__len__` (the `len(obj) > 0` check will not raise an exception)
   * Sequence means that it has a `__len__` and a `__getitem__`. The `__getitem__` is not required by any other checks in the predicate or any steps in the body.

So just replace `isinstance(obj, Sequence)` with `(isinstance(obj, Iterable) and isinstance(obj, Sized))`; the `Sized` symbol can be imported the same way that `Sequence` is.

The [difference between](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/19198166/whats-the-difference-between-a-module-and-a-library-in-python) `library`, `package` and `module` is subtle, but I think it is useful when talking about this kind of namespace management.  The goal of the `__init__.py` module in a `library` is to create a `package` with a namespace that includes `subpackages` and attributes from `subpackages`. Technically `module` is a Python object, though it is often used to refer to a file containing the code for this object.  To help organize modules and provide a naming hierarchy, Python has a concept of packages.  As a rough analogy, modules are like files while packages are like directories. The `__init__.py` module in the package determines the namespace for the package. The term subpackage is used similarly to the term subdirectory for file systems.  I'm thinking that a module is defined in a single file, a package is held in a directory with an `__init__.py` file to load more than one module.  That package could then be a subpackage of another package, etc.  In the current code the term `module` is used to refer to many different kinds of objects -- though they are technically all python objects, the distinction between code from a single file and code from a directory is useful here.

module <-> python file
package <-> python directory (with __init__.py file)
subpackage <-> python directory within another python directory (like subdirectory)

So, for the name of the `attach` function, I like `lazy.package_structure`.  That is, this function constructs the package structure for this package. We give it a name for the package, a list of subpackages and attributes to include in the namespace and it lazily adds those entries to the namespace. 

The name is definitely longer than `attach`, and it is not a verb, so maybe there is a better name. But by not being a verb, it makes clear that this is where we set up the structure for the namespace of this package.

Hi, @szha .
The reason of "scalar array would trigger the problem" is:
https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/835e25031f847b80277b6d11db0519723d26a80a/src/operator/nn/activation.cc#L126-L140
- If the input `x` is scalar array, `SupportMKLDNNAct(param, inputs[0])` will `return false`.
- If the input `x` is vector array, `SupportMKLDNNAct(param, inputs[0])` will `return true`. And the function `MKLDNNActivationBackward` will make it work. Maybe the following code take effect.
https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/835e25031f847b80277b6d11db0519723d26a80a/src/operator/nn/mkldnn/mkldnn_act.cc#L266-L272

There are 2 solutions to make scalar array input to work.
1. The input of `log_sigmoid_grad` should be `y`. So we can modify the following code which takes `x` as its input. This is also what I am doing in this pr.
https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/835e25031f847b80277b6d11db0519723d26a80a/src/operator/mshadow_op.h#L416
      ```c++
      MXNET_UNARY_MATH_OP(log_sigmoid_grad, 1.0f - math::exp(a));
      ```
2. Since `log_sigmoid_grad` takes `x` as input, we can also change the following code. Now it will make `x` as input to `log_sigmoid_grad`.
https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/835e25031f847b80277b6d11db0519723d26a80a/src/operator/nn/activation-inl.h#L207-L210
    ```c++
        case activation::kLogSigmoid:
          ActivationBackward<xpu, mshadow_op::log_sigmoid, mshadow_op::log_sigmoid_grad>(
              ctx, inputs[0], inputs[2], req[0], outputs[0]);
          break;
    ```

I think solution_1 is better. For `y = log_sigmoid(x)`, it calculates dx based on `(dy, y)` instead of `(dy, x)` which enables inplace operation during `y = log_simoid(x)` (i.e. y and x shares the same memory).

Another problem arose when I adopted the solution_1. The gradient of `sym.log_sigmoid()` will be wrong. The reason of this problem is that the input of `_backward_log_sigmoid` is `x`. When I adopt the solution_1, the input of `_backward_log_sigmoid` should be `y`.  The source code of `sym.log_sigmoid()` is the following.
https://github.com/apache/incubator-mxnet/blob/835e25031f847b80277b6d11db0519723d26a80a/src/operator/tensor/elemwise_unary_op_basic.cc#L152-L167
So, I change it to `ElemwiseGradUseOut` in reference of the source code of `sym.sigmoid()`.

I am comparing each headers into lowercase because it might be possible that user add headers in first-letter capitals. Also presigned headers are going to be present in the presigned URL (like `'Content-Type': 'application/json'` is going to be present in url as` '&content-type=application/json'`. We are giving priority to the request header's values not those headers who are in query parameter. So whatever we are getting in request's headers we will consider that while calculating the signature (as AWS works). Also there is a case that we might add some headers while generating presign url but not when we actually we hit that url. In that case we are checking that if key is present or not in headers, if it is then we are ignoring those headers (as per request header's value are more important than the query parameter header's value). So to making that comparison we need to make it case-insensitive.

I started with the CHX profile collection implementation as a base (the variable names are the same for the Signals shared with that implementation), so it should be a superset of that class. I just added signals for all the remaining PVs defined by the Eiger driver.

The file saving in Eiger that doesn't use the default file plugins is common to all Eiger detectors. Basically, since the detector is too fast to follow the normal plugin chain, the vendor API writes to a super-fast cache first, and then this built in file plugin in the eiger driver asynchronously copies the saved hdf5 files from the cache to the target location specified by the filepath PV.

This actually isn't limited to just the Eiger driver - there are several detectors that use File PVs to control built in vendor API file saving operations, skipping the typical areaDetector plugin chain. The giveaway is basically if the `NDFile.template` file is included in the driver database definition:

https://github.com/areaDetector/ADEiger/blob/01bb57cc2d1e3d78fbf56f88c38406be3e61574e/eigerApp/Db/eiger.template#L6

Basically to account for this, in a previous PR I had split the definition of the PVs defined in `NDFile.template` out from the `FilePlugin` class into the new `FileBase` class, and these signals should cover you for the one-off file plugin wrapper signals - they will now just be under the `EigerDetectorCam` umbrella, so that one-off class should theoretically be no longer needed. Instead, the stage/unstage code should probably be moved into a subclass of `EigerDetector`.

I think that overall what would be the most beneficial here in upstream is code to avoid the boilerplating required to create a usable ophyd object for an areaDetector, I believe described in an issue here: https://github.com/bluesky/ophyd/issues/889 and https://github.com/bluesky/ophyd/issues/888

Generally all areaDetectors use the same plugin format, so maybe helper functions that automatically add all the plugins loaded by default by [`commonPlugins.cmd`](https://github.com/areaDetector/ADCore/blob/master/iocBoot/EXAMPLE_commonPlugins.cmd) would be a nice start?

3. stores it as a value in the `ThreadDict`.

That is not possible:
Because a gateway can be supplied to the init of the `SubDevice` class, there is a argument type `Gateway`.
Therefore at the top of the `devices.py` file the `Gateway` class is imported from the "gateway.py" file.

Therefore somthing from "devices.py" can only be imported from the "gateway.py" file after the line in which the  `Gateway` class is defined, otherwise you create a cyclic import (a loop that will endlessly jump between the files to try and import something from one another. (same thing holds for the `GatewayException` class).

It can therefore only be moved up (but below the `GatewayException` class) if the type definition is removed from the `__init__` of the `SubDevice` class.

So kind of like you can see with the `_AlgebraicDataclass` mixin introduced in #8437, while we can revert to mypy-compatible typing, we can move the extensions made *on top of* more standard python types into mixins (e.g. `pants.util.objects.DatatypeMixin` or `ChoicesMixin`. One of the things that `.resolve_for_enum_variant()` has that this still loses is exhaustiveness checking -- as in, that *only* the correct keys are provided, and that *all* cases are accounted for. It also means we *don't* have to ever make these types of refactorings if python 4 introduces a new type of dataclasses, for example (**stable API!!!**). Could we consider keeping the `ChoicesMixin`, specializing it into a `StdlibEnumWrapperMixin` for stdlib enums to implement `.resolve_for_enum_variant()`, and then having all the new stdlib `Enum` classes also mix that in, and then possibly avoid having to make these changes?

Dynamic providers typically don't work correctly when used from multi-language components (#5455). Somehow the way we were previously setting up the tests to use the shared pipenv virtualenv allowed the Python MLC tests that used dynamic providers to pass. But when I changed the tests to use their own virtual env from (python -m venv venv) I was running into errors along the lines of #5455.

Instead of trying to rework how we setup the tests to get them to pass, I changed the Python MLCs to use a resource from a testprovider rather than a resource from a dynamic provider inside the MLC. The testprovider previously only exposed a `Random` resource and as part of this change I added a new `Echo` resource. These changes were just some refactoring to more cleanly expose both `Random` and `Echo` from the testprovider.

With these changes, we could potentially do some further subsequent cleanup of the MLC tests:

- There are some tests with a MLC implemented in TypeScript that use a dynamic provider, and we have some hacks in the integration test package to make this work. But it is ugly and not representative of real-world usage (you have to have a `node_modules` dir in your program directory with the dependencies needed by the MLC, even if your program  is written in another language). We could remove those hacks and replace the use of the dynamic provider with the new `Echo` resource in the testprovider.

- Go doesn't have dynamic providers and in order to implement the equivalent MLC in Go we had to expose an equivalent `Echo` resource from the MLC provider itself (this was before I had added the testprovider). We could instead clean these Go test MLCs up to not do that, which would significantly reduce the amount of code in the MLC, and instead use the new `Echo` resource in the testprovider.

- When we get around to implementing support for authoring MLC in .NET, we'll be able to use the new `Echo` resource in the testprovider.

I want to draw reviewers' attention to this block. This setup checks for ports to use sequentially. So it will first run `_find_open_port()` on worker 1's machine, then worker 2's machine, then work 3's, etc.

That means there is a small startup time introduced. I saw that this process took about 5 seconds per worker when I was running a cluster on AWS Fargate. So that means you might expect around 1 minute of startup time for a cluster with 20 workers.

I don't think this is too bad, and I think it's a fairly clean way to ensure that if you have multiple work processes on the same machine, they're assigned different ports in the network parameters

out of curiosity, why you decided for this way to get the username. 
Here two function calls are needed, while there are possibilities with just one call, e.g. with the `getpass` library: `getpass.getuser( )`. 
The additional advantage here is also that this function first checks for commen env variables (`USERNAME`, `USER`, `LOGNAME`) and only if they are not set it will use the `pwd` module. 
The pwd module will directly do something similar as `getent passwd`. This is fine on systems with only local user entries. But can be more problematic on systems with e.g. LDAP, because it will increase the LDAP queries (if it is not cached) and will also not work if there any problems with LDAP.

In following example I disabled LDAP to simulate a LDAP error:
```
>>> import getpass
>>> getpass.getuser()
'hille'
>>> import os, pwd
>>> pwd.getpwuid(os.getuid())[0]
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
KeyError: 'getpwuid(): uid not found: 28242'
```

While `getpass.getuser()` is still working, `pwd` is not working anymore.

If an object with a custom `eq` is mutable, it really shouldn't be hashable, unless the custom equality and hash are based on some immutable subset of the attributes. If we want hashability, we need to make the instances immutable. If we nevertheless still want to "edit" such instances, we can add methods that make a _copy_ of the instance containing the changes (IIRC the Retry objects from API core are an example of that).

Right now there's three options:
1. Use `IO[bytes]`
2. Use `SpooledTemporaryFile` directly
3. Write a protocol for just the methods we need

Source: https://github.com/python/typing/discussions/829#discussioncomment-1150451

I don't think we should choose (2) and I think we should change our docs. Let's not lock ourselves into concrete implementations if we don't need to.

I think (1) has issues (as mentioned in that discussion and others) but is widely used so it's pretty defensible if a user had to add a `# type: ignore` to that parameter if they pass in some less common file-like object.

(3) is the _correct_ option but also the most boilerplate / overhead for us. It would look like:

```python
class FileLike(Protocol):
    def write(self, __data: bytes) -> None:  ...
    def read(self, __n: int) -> bytes:  ...
    def seek(self, __pos: int) -> None:  ...
    def close(self) -> None:  ...
```

1. Client registers the actor to the GCS.
2. Client reports actor dependencies ready.
3. GCS schedules the actor.

So the method should be named after these steps. If we name this method `CreateActor`, it could be confusing because the first step (register) is also part of the actor creation steps.

Also, in the future, we can add an optimization to skip step 2 if depdencies are already ready at step 1. At that time, this name would become even more weird.

I'll preface this with the statement that I won't block this. But I don't agree with this particular way of doing the release notes and without time-pressure or the feeling that I may be missing something I would veto this. I'm sorry for not speaking up earlier on this, but from previous discussions I didn't think that the goal was to be this terse in the notes. I get the argument that creating elaborate release notes is work and shouldn't be taken to an extreme. But I also worry that this may be going to much into the other extreme. 

I think release notes are documentation that is useful to us and to users who are curious about changes between releases and want to discover new features and be aware of deprecated ones. How else are they supposed to find out? And with regard to users, I really don't want to make them read PRs. Especially because those may contain anything from outdated descriptions to discussions that are to large to read. PRs also don't necessarily correspond to a good bullet point in the release notes. Changes belonging to the same effort may be split across PRs or PRs may update independent things that deserve independent entries in the notes. 

I think the line I'm commenting on here is a good example of my critique. What is a user supposed to learn from "Unify pseudo-random seeding interface"? There is no mention of refactoring to `rng` and the actual change is split among two PRs of which the latter isn't mentioned here (https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/pull/6924). At this level, release notes cease being useful and are just a fancy reference list of development steps that may or may not have useful titles. 

I also want to point out that this is deviating from what SciPy, NumPy and other packages in the ecosystem do. Is our changes / maintainer resources ratio really that much higher than theirs to warrant this trade-off? 

I'm not sure how time-critical you think this is but I'm willing to go over these over the weekend and try to arrive at a compromise if you are willing to wait for that. And as always, I mean this is the most constructive way possible and are of course open to arguments. :)

If that host has a disk full condition, you'd just change that environment variable manually. This is what you can do via `/etc/pexrc`.

When you think about the details here - unless I'm missing something, a disk can go full at any time, even in between when Pex 1st checks for the condition and later when it goes to write files during a single run of a PEX. You can never fully avoid that race condition. You can check early in a PEX run whether a disk is _already_ full - which I think is what you're trying to do - but that's still best effort if the answer is no. Now as far as getting an answer yes - the disk is full - how do you propose that is done? I have no clue how you do this except by something hacky like: assume the system has the `df` binary on the path and use it to get the current full state of the disks on the system and then map the given pex root to one of those disks by checking the mount prefix that contains it. That seems super hacky. Do you have some other idea?

This branch is intended to inverse the order of the `digitize` result in order to comply with the descending order being set:
```python
>>> col_to_digitize = [1, 2, 3, 4]
>>> ascending = False
>>> pivots = [3.0, 3.0]
>>> groupby_col = np.digitize(col_to_digitize, pivots)
array([0, 0, 1, 1], dtype=int64)
>>> split_col_into_bins(col_to_digitize, groupby_col)
[[1, 2], [3, 4]] # these are in ascending order, however we wanted descending
>>> groupby_col = len(pivots) - groupby_col
array([1, 1, 0, 0], dtype=int64)
>>> split_col_into_bins(col_to_digitize, groupby_col)
[[3, 4], [1, 2]] # now they're in the order we wanted
```

At the first glance, there's nothing wrong with entering this branch if `len(pivots == 1)`. cc @RehanSD

Because the other transformations look somewhat like

```py
def forward(sample):
    for transform in transforms:
        sample = apply_recursively(transform, sample)
        
    return sample
```

whereas AugMix looks like

```py
def forward(sample):
    for item in sample:
        items = []
        for transform in transforms:
            items.append(transform(item))

        item = mix(items)

    return sample
```

IIUC, we cannot apply the transformations sequentially to the whole sample, but rather need to apply all of them to each item in the sample individually and mix the results together.

Allowing views to choose a 'default color' for non-matching runs is reasonable, however I would suggest making the state explicitly hold either a default color or `null` instead of empty strings.

Using an empty string means that all consumers of `getRunColorMap` **must** prefer doing `colorMap[runId] || defaultColor` instead of `colorMap[runId] ?? defaultColor`, if they want to show a default color for non-matching runs.  Making it a requirement for views to handle a `null` is slightly more obvious than an empty string I think, because TS will hopefully complain when a view tries to do `let x: string = colorMap[runId]` without handling the `null` case.

If we'd like to proceed with empty strings, could we
- Check that all consumers [1] handle the empty string, and update cases like ScalarCardContainer [2] to use `colorMap[runId] || defaultColor`
- Add comments in https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/blob/master/tensorboard/webapp/runs/store/runs_types.ts#L50, explaining that the `defaultRunColorForGroupBy` field may contain empty string values for non-matching runs, and that `runColorOverrideForGroupBy` may not contain empty string values
- Update the comment on `getRunColorMap` [3], so that future consumers may realize that the map may contain empty string values

Or if you prefer using `null`, could we update the selector's comment to describe what a `null` value means?

Or if you prefer keeping a default color in the state, then it guarantees runs to have the same color across all views, and no further action needed.

Wdyt?

[1] https://cs.opensource.google/search?q=%22getRunColorMap)%22&sq=&ss=tensorflow%2Ftensorboard
[2] https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/blob/master/tensorboard/webapp/metrics/views/card_renderer/scalar_card_container.ts#L380
[3] https://github.com/tensorflow/tensorboard/blob/master/tensorboard/webapp/runs/store/runs_selectors.ts#L189

My view on this is that if/when the definition of service data changes (new fields are added), it is the responsibility of new code to handle this. If the new code continues to use `sizeof()` then it is a bug in new code. 

We can define `kMinLength` today and use it in the code to try to protect for future changes but it does not add much protection since the new code needs to anyways handle the additional fields as optional (check against different lengths as parsing the data). 

The main reason I would say we, in general, prefer and use `sizeof()` over defining a constant value directly is that complier is better at getting the size over people (compliers hopefully makes less mistake 😄).

OSD is very special, because it does _not_ use multiple models, but needs a single model with multiple scripts – for which `osd` is of course the most versatile, but also `frk` contains both `Latin` and `Fraktur`, `hin` contains some `Latin` as well etc.)

I followed the same pattern as the UDP Socket API. For UDP sockets, what OpenThread currently does is provide the context pointer associated with the socket in the callback, rather than a pointer to the socket itself. That's what the `aContext` argument is doing here.

I think that we should stick to using `aContext` rather than providing a pointer to the socket directly, at the very least because that's what OpenThread's UDP socket seems to do. I think the reason the UDP API does this is because a different context (e.g., some other struct which in turn contains a pointer to the socket) might be more useful to the application developer in some cases. But, I am happy to be convinced otherwise if others feel strongly that we should switch to a pointer to the socket for TCP. :)

No, users should never see this from our first-party globus-sdk behaviors. We are no longer setting `message` directly, so the SDK never triggers this codepath (except in the test for this).

The way it would likely happen is with third-party tooling built on top of the SDK which sets `message`. Or, if the user is maintaining that tooling, it's their own second-party code using globus-sdk.

Consider this one:

```python
class MyError(globus_sdk.GlobusAPIError):
    def __init__(self, r):
        super().__init__(r)
        self.message = "For the lulz"
```

That error class will emit the warning. If the maintainer is behaving as we hope and has some testing environment where they see those warnings (e.g. pytest), they'll see the warning when they test with the new SDK version.
